name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
فَإِذَا نُفِخَ فِى ٱلصُّورِ نَفْخَةٌ وَٰحِدَةٌ (١٣)
```

```english
When the Trumpet is blown with a single blast (13)
```

```arabic
وَحُمِلَتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ وَٱلْجِبَالُ فَدُكَّتَا دَكَّةً وَٰحِدَةً (١٤)
```

```english
and the earth and the mountains are lifted and levelled with a single levelling, (14)
```

```arabic
فَيَوْمَئِذٍ وَقَعَتِ ٱلْوَاقِعَةُ (١٥)
```

```english
then, on that day, will the Imminent [Hour] befall (15)
```

```arabic
وَٱنشَقَّتِ ٱلسَّمَآءُ فَهِىَ يَوْمَئِذٍ وَاهِيَةٌ (١٦)
```

```english
and the sky will be split open—for it will be frail on that day— (16)
```

```arabic
وَٱلْمَلَكُ عَلَىٰٓ أَرْجَآئِهَا ۚ وَيَحْمِلُ عَرْشَ رَبِّكَ فَوْقَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ ثَمَـٰنِيَةٌ (١٧)
```

```english
with the angels all over it, and the Throne of your Lord will be borne that day by eight [angels]. (17)
```

```arabic
يَوْمَئِذٍ تُعْرَضُونَ لَا تَخْفَىٰ مِنكُمْ خَافِيَةٌ (١٨)
```

```english
That day you will be presented [before your Lord]: none of your secrets will remain hidden. (18)
```

```arabic
فَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِىَ كِتَـٰبَهُۥ بِيَمِينِهِۦ فَيَقُولُ هَآؤُمُ ٱقْرَءُوا۟ كِتَـٰبِيَهْ (١٩)
```

```english
As for him who is given his book in his right hand, he will say, ‘Here, take and read my book! (19)
```

```arabic
إِنِّى ظَنَنتُ أَنِّى مُلَـٰقٍ حِسَابِيَهْ (٢٠)
```

```english
Indeed I knew that I will encounter my account [of deeds].’ (20)
```

```arabic
فَهُوَ فِى عِيشَةٍ رَّاضِيَةٍ (٢١)
```

```english
So he will have a pleasant life, (21)
```

```arabic
فِى جَنَّةٍ عَالِيَةٍ (٢٢)
```

```english
in an elevated garden, (22)
```

```arabic
قُطُوفُهَا دَانِيَةٌ (٢٣)
```

```english
whose clusters [of fruits] will be within easy reach. (23)
```

```arabic
كُلُوا۟ وَٱشْرَبُوا۟ هَنِيٓـًٔۢا بِمَآ أَسْلَفْتُمْ فِى ٱلْأَيَّامِ ٱلْخَالِيَةِ (٢٤)
```

```english
[He will be told]: ‘Enjoy your food and drink, for what you had sent in advance in past days [for your future life].’ (24)
```

```arabic
وَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِىَ كِتَـٰبَهُۥ بِشِمَالِهِۦ فَيَقُولُ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى لَمْ أُوتَ كِتَـٰبِيَهْ (٢٥)
```

```english
But as for him who is given his book in his left hand, he will say, ‘I wish I had not been given my book, (25)
```

```arabic
وَلَمْ أَدْرِ مَا حِسَابِيَهْ (٢٦)
```

```english
nor had I ever known what my account is! (26)
```

```arabic
يَـٰلَيْتَهَا كَانَتِ ٱلْقَاضِيَةَ (٢٧)
```

```english
I wish death had been the end of it all! (27)
```

```arabic
مَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنِّى مَالِيَهْ ۜ (٢٨)
```

```english
My wealth did not avail me. (28)
```

```arabic
هَلَكَ عَنِّى سُلْطَـٰنِيَهْ (٢٩)
```

```english
My authority has left me.’ (29)
```

```arabic
خُذُوهُ فَغُلُّوهُ (٣٠)
```

```english
[The angels will be told:] ‘Seize him, and fetter him! (30)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ ٱلْجَحِيمَ صَلُّوهُ (٣١)
```

```english
Then put him into hell. (31)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ فِى سِلْسِلَةٍ ذَرْعُهَا سَبْعُونَ ذِرَاعًا فَٱسْلُكُوهُ (٣٢)
```

```english
Then bind him in a chain, seventy cubits in length. (32)
```

```arabic
إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ لَا يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٣٣)
```

```english
Indeed he had no faith in Allah, the All-supreme, (33)
```

```arabic
وَلَا يَحُضُّ عَلَىٰ طَعَامِ ٱلْمِسْكِينِ (٣٤)
```

```english
and he did not urge the feeding of the needy, (34)
```

```arabic
فَلَيْسَ لَهُ ٱلْيَوْمَ هَـٰهُنَا حَمِيمٌ (٣٥)
```

```english
so he has no friend here today, (35)
```

```arabic
وَلَا طَعَامٌ إِلَّا مِنْ غِسْلِينٍ (٣٦)
```

```english
nor any food except pus, (36)
```

```arabic
لَّا يَأْكُلُهُۥٓ إِلَّا ٱلْخَـٰطِـُٔونَ (٣٧)
```

```english
which no one shall eat except the iniquitous.’ (37)
```

## (Commentary)

This is the second section of the verses, defining the Inevitable (الحاقة) — *the reality of the Resurrection that must surely come to pass* — by some of its signs and a portion of what occurs therein.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَإِذا نُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ نَفْخَةٌ واحِدَةٌ** **"When the Trumpet is blown with a single blast"** (13). It has been previously mentioned that the blowing of the Trumpet (الصور) is an allusion to the Resurrection and the summoning for the final judgment. Describing the blast as "single" (واحدة) points to the swift execution of the command and the efficacy of Divine Power (القدرة); there is no weakness in it such that it would require a repetition of the blast. What immediately comes to mind from the context of the verses is that this is the second blast, which brings the dead back to life.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَحُمِلَتِ الْأَرْضُ وَالْجِبالُ فَدُكَّتا دَكَّةً واحِدَةً** **"and the earth and the mountains are lifted and leveled with a single leveling"** (14). Crushing (الدك) is the most intense form of pounding, which is to break a thing and transform it into small fragments. The "lifting" (حمل) of the earth and mountains refers to the encompassment of Divine Power over them. Describing the leveling as "single" indicates the speed of their disintegration, such that it does not require a second leveling.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَيَوْمَئِذٍ وَقَعَتِ الْواقِعَةُ** **"On that day the Imminent Event will come to pass"** (15). Meaning, the Resurrection will be established.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَانْشَقَّتِ السَّماءُ فَهِيَ يَوْمَئِذٍ واهِيَةٌ** **"and the sky will be split open, for it will be frail on that day"** (16). The splitting (انشقاق) of a thing is the separation of one part of it from another. Frail (واهية) is derived from *al-wahy* (الوهي), meaning weakness. It is also said to be from *al-wahy* in the sense of a tear in leather, a garment, or the like.

It is possible that this verse—meaning His saying: **وَانْشَقَّتِ السَّماءُ فَهِيَ يَوْمَئِذٍ واهِيَةٌ وَالْمَلَكُ عَلى أَرْجائِها**—carries the same meaning as His saying: **وَيَوْمَ تَشَقَّقُ السَّماءُ بِالْغَمامِ وَنُزِّلَ الْمَلائِكَةُ تَنْزِيلاً** **"The day when the sky will be split open with the clouds and the angels will be sent down in a [majestic] descent"** (25:25).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالْمَلَكُ عَلى أَرْجائِها وَيَحْمِلُ عَرْشَ رَبِّكَ فَوْقَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ ثَمانِيَةٌ** **"with the angels all over its sides, and on that day eight [angels] will bear the Throne of your Lord above them"** (17). Al-Raghib said: "The *raja* (رجا) of a well or the sky or anything else is its side, and the plural is *arja’* (أرجاء). The Exalted said: **وَالْمَلَكُ عَلى أَرْجائِها**." End quote. The term "angel" (الملك) — as has been said — can refer to a single angel or a collective, and what is intended in the verse is the collective.

Regarding His saying: **وَيَحْمِلُ عَرْشَ رَبِّكَ فَوْقَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ ثَمانِيَةٌ**, the pronoun in "above them" (فَوْقَهُمْ) refers to the angels, according to the apparent requirement of the context. It has also been said that the pronoun refers to the created beings (الخلائق).

The apparent sense of His speech is that the Throne (العرش) — *the locus of Divine command and authority* — has bearers from among the angels today, as the Exalted said: **الَّذِينَ يَحْمِلُونَ الْعَرْشَ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهُ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ وَيُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** **"Those who bear the Throne, and those who are around it, celebrate the praise of their Lord and have faith in Him, and they plead for forgiveness for the faithful"** (40:7). Narrations have stated that they are four. The apparent sense of the verse—meaning His saying: **وَيَحْمِلُ عَرْشَ رَبِّكَ فَوْقَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ ثَمانِيَةٌ**—is that the bearers on the Day of Resurrection will be eight. Whether they are from the angels or from others, the verse is silent on that, although the context is not devoid of an indication that they are from the angels.

It is possible—as previously hinted—that the purpose of mentioning the splitting of the sky, the angels being on its sides, and the bearers of the Throne being eight on that day, is to describe the manifestation of the angels, the sky, and the Throne to man on that day. The Exalted said: **وَتَرَى الْمَلائِكَةَ حَافِّينَ مِنْ حَوْلِ الْعَرْشِ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ** **"And you will see the angels surrounding the Throne, celebrating the praise of their Lord"** (39:75).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يَوْمَئِذٍ تُعْرَضُونَ لا تَخْفى مِنْكُمْ خافِيَةٌ** **"On that day you will be exposed: none of your secrets will remain hidden"** (18). It is apparent that what is intended is the presentation (العرض) before Allah, as the Exalted said: **وَعُرِضُوا عَلى رَبِّكَ صَفًّا** **"and they shall be presented before your Lord in ranks"** (18:48). Presentation (العرض) is like a seller showing his goods to a buyer by spreading them out before him. Thus, the presentation on that day before Allah—which is the Day of Judgment—is the bringing forth of what man possesses of belief and action in a manner that no hidden belief or hidden deed remains concealed. This occurs by the transformation of the Unseen (الغيب) into the witnessed (شهادة) and the secret into the manifest. He said: **يَوْمَ تُبْلَى السَّرائِرُ** **"on the day when the secrets are examined"** (86:9), and He said: **يَوْمَ هُمْ بارِزُونَ لا يَخْفى عَلَى اللهِ مِنْهُمْ شَيْءٌ** **"the day when they will emerge [from their graves]: nothing about them will be hidden from Allah"** (40:16).

It has been established in our previous discussions that those things counted in the speech of the Exalted as characteristics of the Day of Resurrection—such as the exclusivity of sovereignty to Allah, the command belonging to Him, the absence of any protector from Him, the emergence of creation before Him, and the fact that nothing of them is hidden from Him—all of these are eternally established for the Exalted. What is intended is the manifestation of these realities on that day with a clarity that leaves no veil or doubt.

The meaning, then, is: On that day it will become manifest that you are in a state of presentation before the knowledge of Allah, and every hidden deed among your actions will be revealed.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ فَيَقُولُ هاؤُمُ اقْرَؤُا كِتابِيَهْ** **"As for him who is given his book in his right hand, he will say, ‘Here, take and read my book!"** (19). It is stated in *al-Majma‘*: "**هاؤم** is a command for a group, equivalent to *hakum* (هاكم). You say to one: *ha’a* (هاء) O man; to two: *ha’uma* (هاؤما); to a group: *ha’um* (هاؤم); to a woman: *ha’i* (هاء) with a *kasrah* on the *hamzah* and no *ya’* after it; to two women: *ha’uma*; and to a group of women: *ha’unna*. This is the dialect of the people of Hijaz.

The tribes of Tamim and Qays say: *ha’a* (هاء) for a man like the people of Hijaz; for two: *ha’a* (هاءا); for a group: *ha’u* (هاؤا); for a woman: *ha’i* (هائي); and for women: *ha’unna*.

Some Arabs place a *kaf* in place of the *hamzah*, saying: *hak*, *hakuma*, *hakum*, *haki*, *hakuma*, *hakunna*. Its meaning is: 'Take' or 'receive'. It is used as a command and not as a prohibition." End quote.

This verse and those following it until His saying: **الْخاطِؤُنَ** (the sinners) provide a detailed explanation of the differing states of people on that day in terms of felicity (السعادة) and wretchedness (الشقاء). A discussion on the meaning of being given the book in the right hand has already passed in the commentary of His saying: **فَمَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ** (17:71). It is apparent that His saying: **هاؤُمُ اقْرَؤُا كِتابِيَهْ** is an address to the angels. The *ha’* in **كِتابِيَهْ**, as well as at the ends of the subsequent verses, is for the purpose of pausing (الوقف) and is called the *ha’* of rest (هاء الاستراحة).

The meaning is: As for him who is given his book in his right hand, he says to the angels: "Take and read my book," meaning it is a book that decrees my felicity.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنِّي ظَنَنْتُ أَنِّي مُلاقٍ حِسابِيَهْ** **"Indeed I knew that I will encounter my account"** (20). Here, "thought" (الظن) carries the meaning of certainty (اليقين). The verse serves as a justification for what is gained from the previous verse. The essence of the justification is: My book was only the book of the right hand, decreeing my felicity, because I was certain in the world that I would encounter my account; thus, I believed in my Lord and rectified my deeds.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَهُوَ فِي عِيشَةٍ راضِيَةٍ** **"So he will be in a pleasant life"** (21). Meaning, he lives a life that he is pleased with. Attributing "pleasure" (الرضا) to the "life" (العيشة) is a form of intellectual metaphor (المجاز العقلي).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فِي جَنَّةٍ عالِيَةٍ** **"in a lofty garden"** (22) — until His saying — **الْخالِيَةِ** **"bygone [days]"** (24). Meaning, he is in a garden lofty in status, containing that which no eye has seen, no ear has heard, and has never occurred to the heart of any human.

His saying: **قُطُوفُها دانِيَةٌ** **"with its clusters [of fruits] within reach"** (23). *Qutuf* (القطوف) is the plural of *qitf* (قطف), which refers to the fruit that is harvested. The meaning is: its fruits are near to him, and he may take them however he wishes.

His saying: **كُلُوا وَاشْرَبُوا هَنِيئاً بِما أَسْلَفْتُمْ فِي الْأَيَّامِ الْخالِيَةِ** **"‘Eat and drink healthfully for what you had sent ahead in bygone days’"** (24). Meaning, it is said to them: Eat and drink from all that is eaten and drunk therein, in a state of being healthful (هنيئا) for you, because of the faith and righteous deeds you sent forward in the world whose days have passed.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِشِمالِهِ فَيَقُولُ يا لَيْتَنِي لَمْ أُوتَ كِتابِيَهْ وَلَمْ أَدْرِ ما حِسابِيَهْ** **"But as for him who is given his book in his left hand, he will say, ‘I wish I had not been given my book, and had not known what my account is!"** (25-26). These are the second group, the wretched criminals. They are given the record of their deeds in their left hand. The discussion on its meaning has passed in Surah al-Isra’. These people wish they had not been given their book nor known their account; they wish this because of the painful punishment they witness prepared for them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا لَيْتَها كانَتِ الْقاضِيَةَ** **"I wish it had been the end!"** (27). They have mentioned that the pronoun in "it" (لَيْتَها) refers to the first death that man tasted in the world.

The meaning is: I wish the first death I tasted had been the final end (القاضية) for me, decreeing my non-existence, so that I would have perished and not been resurrected alive to fall into the abyss of eternal punishment and witness what I witness.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ما أَغْنى عَنِّي مالِيَهْ هَلَكَ عَنِّي سُلْطانِيَهْ** **"My wealth has not availed me. My authority has departed from me"** (28-29). These are two expressions of intense regret (تحسر) that he utters when he sees the failure of his efforts in the world. He used to think that the key to his happiness in life was wealth and authority, which would repel every dislikeable thing from him and give him power over everything he loved and desired. Thus, he exerted all his effort in acquiring them, turned away from his Lord and from every truth to which he was called, and denied his caller. When he witnesses the severing of all means and that he is in a day when neither wealth nor sons avail, he mentions the uselessness of his wealth and the vanishing of his authority in regret and pain. But what use is regret?

**His saying, the Exalted:** **خُذُوهُ فَغُلُّوهُ** **"‘Seize him and fetter him"** (30) — until His saying — **فَاسْلُكُوهُ** **"and then chain him"** (32). This is a narration of the command of the Exalted to the angels to seize him and enter him into the Fire. The implied meaning is: It is said to the angels, "Seize him," etc. **فَغُلُّوهُ** is a command from *al-ghull* (الغل), which is to bind with a shackle (غل) that joins the hand, the foot, and the neck.

His saying: **ثُمَّ الْجَحِيمَ صَلُّوهُ** **"then put him into hell"** (31). Meaning, enter him into the Great Fire and make him endure it.

His saying: **ثُمَّ فِي سِلْسِلَةٍ ذَرْعُها سَبْعُونَ ذِراعاً فَاسْلُكُوهُ** **"then chain him in a chain whose length is seventy cubits"** (32). The chain (السلسلة) is the fetter. The *dhar‘* (الذرع) is the length. The cubit (الذراع) is the distance between the elbow and the tip of the fingers, which is a unit of length. To "chain him" (سلوكه فيه) means to place him within it. The sum of the meaning is: Then place him in a fetter whose length is seventy cubits.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّهُ كانَ لا يُؤْمِنُ بِاللهِ الْعَظِيمِ وَلا يَحُضُّ عَلى طَعامِ الْمِسْكِينِ** **"Indeed he had no faith in Allah, the All-supreme, and he did not urge the feeding of the needy"** (33-34). Urging (الحض) is to incite and encourage. These two verses serve as the justification for the command to seize and enter him into the Fire. That is, the seizing, then the burning in Hell, and the chaining in the chain are because he did not believe in Allah, the All-supreme, and did not urge the feeding of the needy; meaning, he was negligent regarding the affairs of the poor and indifferent to what they suffered.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَلَيْسَ لَهُ الْيَوْمَ هاهُنا حَمِيمٌ** **"So today he has no loyal friend here"** (35) — until His saying — **الْخاطِؤُنَ** **"the sinners"** (37). The loyal friend (الحميم) is the close friend. The verse is a corollary to His saying: **إِنَّهُ كانَ لا يُؤْمِنُ** etc. The sum of the meaning is: Since he did not believe in Allah, the All-supreme, he has no friend here today who can benefit him—meaning an intercessor (شفيع) to intercede for him—for there is no forgiveness for a disbeliever, and thus no intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah*.

His saying: **وَلا طَعامٌ إِلَّا مِنْ غِسْلِينٍ** **"nor any food except pus"** (36). *Ghislin* (الغسلين) is the wash-water; what is intended is that which flows from the bodies of the people of the Fire, such as pus and the like. The verse is conjoined to His saying in the previous verse: **حَمِيمٌ**, and is a corollary to His saying: **وَلا يَحُضُ** etc. The sum of the meaning is: Since he did not urge the feeding of the needy, he has no food here today except from the *ghislin* of the people of the Fire.

His saying: **لا يَأْكُلُهُ إِلَّا الْخاطِؤُنَ** **"which none but the sinners eat"** (37). This is a description of the *ghislin*. The sinners (الخاطؤُنَ) are those who are steeped in error and sin.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَيَحْمِلُ عَرْشَ رَبِّكَ فَوْقَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ ثَمانِيَةٌ**, Ibn Jarir narrated from Ibn Zayd that he said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: **يحمله اليوم أربعة ويوم القيامة ثمانية** — "Four bear it today, and on the Day of Resurrection, eight."

**I say:** In the restriction of the bearers in the verse by His saying: **يَوْمَئِذٍ** (on that day), there is an indication—rather, a clear manifestation—that this specific number is exclusive to the Resurrection.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, in another tradition, it is said: "Eight bear it: four from the former ones and four from the latter ones. As for the four from the former ones, they are Noah, Abraham, Moses, and Jesus. As for the four from the latter ones, they are Muhammad, Ali, al-Hasan, and al-Husayn عليه‌السلام."

**I say:** In more than one narration, it is stated that the eight are specific to the Day of Resurrection. In some of them, it is said that the bearers of the Throne—and the Throne is knowledge (العلم)—are "four from us and four from whomsoever Allah wills."

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Abu Basir, from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام, he said: "When it is the Day of Resurrection, every group of people will be called by their Imam—the one who died in their era. If he affirms him, he will be given his book in his right hand, because of His saying: **يَوْمَ نَدْعُوا كُلَّ أُناسٍ بِإِمامِهِمْ** (17:71). So whoever is given his book in his right hand, those are the ones who read their book. The 'right hand' (اليمين) is the affirmation of the Imam, for he is his book which he reads..." until he said: "And whoever denied will be among the companions of the left—those of whom Allah said: **وَأَصْحابُ الشِّمالِ ما أَصْحابُ الشِّمالِ ـ فِي سَمُومٍ وَحَمِيمٍ وَظِلٍّ مِنْ يَحْمُومٍ** (56:41-43) etc."

**I say:** In several narrations, the application of His saying: **فَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ** etc., is to Ali عليه‌السلام, and in some, to him and his followers (شيعته). Likewise, the application of His saying: **وَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِشِمالِهِ** etc., is to his enemies. These are instances of *al-jary* (الجري) — *the application of a verse to its subsequent instances* — rather than primary exegesis.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Hakim narrated and authenticated from Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri, from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, who said: **لو أن دلوا من غسلين يهراق في الدنيا لأنتن بأهل الدنيا** — "If a bucket of *ghislin* were poured into the world, it would cause the people of the world to rot from its stench."

In the same source, al-Bayhaqi narrated in *Shu‘ab al-Iman* from Sa‘sa‘ah ibn Sawhan, who said: A Bedouin came to Ali ibn Abi Talib and said: "How is this word [read]: **لا يأكله إلا الخاطون** (those who walk)? By Allah, everyone walks (*yakhtu*)." Ali smiled and said: "O Bedouin, it is **لا يَأْكُلُهُ إِلَّا الْخاطِؤُنَ** (none but the sinners eat it)." He said: "You have spoken the truth, by Allah, O Commander of the Faithful—Allah would not abandon His servant."

Then Ali turned to Abu al-Aswad and said: "The non-Arabs (الأعاجم) have entered the religion in large numbers, so set down something for the people by which they may be guided to the correctness of their tongues." So he established for them the *raf‘*, the *nasb*, and the *khafd* (the grammatical cases).

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from Ibn Babuwayh in *al-Duru‘ al-Waqiyah*, in a tradition from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: **ولو أن ذراعا من السلسلة التي ذكرها الله في كتابه ـ وضع على جميع جبال الدنيا لذابت عن حرها** — "If a single cubit of the chain that Allah mentioned in His Book were placed upon all the mountains of the world, they would melt from its heat."
